elements.sent-2411-001: We study the Bose-Einstein condensation (BEC) of a free Bose gas under rigid rotation.
elements.sent-2411-002: The aim is to explore the impact of rotation on the thermodynamic quantities associated with BEC, including the Bose-Einstein (BE) transition temperature and condensate fraction.
elements.sent-2411-003: We begin by introducing the rotation in the Lagrangian density of free charged Klein-Gordon fields and determine the corresponding grand canonical partition function at finite temperature, chemical potential, and finite angular velocity.
elements.sent-2411-004: Assuming slow rotation, we derive analytical expressions for the pressure, energy, number, and angular momentum densities of a free Bose gas in nonrelativistic and ultrarelativistic limits in terms of the corresponding fugacities.
elements.sent-2411-005: We then focus on the phenomenon of BEC.
elements.sent-2411-006: We calculate the critical temperature of BEC transition and the condensate fraction in a slowly rotating Bose gas including only particles.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our findings indicate that the critical exponent associated with the BE transition in a rotating gas is lower compared to that in a nonrotating Bose gas.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also determine the fugacity in a rotating Bose gas in the aforementioned limits and examine how rotation affects its temperature dependence, both below and above the critical temperature.
elements.sent-2411-009: By analyzing the behavior of heat capacity at these temperatures, we demonstrate that in a nonrelativistic Bose gas, the rotation transforms the nature of the BE phase transition from a continuous to a discontinuous transition.
elements.sent-2411-010: In general, we find that a nonrelativistic Bose gas under rotation behaves similarly to a nonrotating Bose gas in ultrarelativistic limit.
elements.sent-2411-011: Studying the properties of matter under extreme conditions is one of the most intriguing areas of modern theoretical and experimental physics \cite{rajagopal2018,pisarski2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: It has applications across various branches of physics, ranging from condensed matter to astroparticle physics.
elements.sent-2411-013: These extreme conditions, such as high temperatures and densities, as well as very strong magnetic fields and angular frequencies, are particularly realized in compact stars.
elements.sent-2411-014: These stars are the remnants of massive stars that have experienced gravitational collapse.
elements.sent-2411-015: Among the densest compact stars are neutron stars and white dwarfs, possessing extraordinarily high mass-to-radius ratio.
elements.sent-2411-016: Neutron stars, in particular, are characterized by high densities up to $10^{14 \text{g/cm ^{3}$ in their interior.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: This characteristic, combined with the physics of strong interaction, which involves a rich spectrum of particles and attractive interaction channels, allows for pairing of neutrons, protons, mesons, and quarks (see \cite{Pethick:2015jma for a description of various pairing mechanisms).}
elements.sent-2411-018: Among these particles neutrons, protons, and quarks are fermions and mesons are bosons.
elements.sent-2411-019: Regardless of the statistical nature of the pair constituents, these pairings lead to bosonic bound states, that under appropriate circumstances exhibit a BEC.
elements.sent-2411-020: Other research areas related to BEC include weakly interacting dilute gases in magneto-optical traps and strongly interacting quantum gases in optical lattices \cite{Balaz:2010cn .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, a large fraction of bosons occupy the lowest quantum state.
elements.sent-2411-022: As temperature increases this condensate fraction decreases until it eventually vanishes at a certain critical temperature.
elements.sent-2411-023: The latter is given in terms of the total number density of bosons and their thermal de Broglie wavelength (or thermal length).
elements.sent-2411-024: This critical temperature corresponds to the point at which the thermal length and the average interparticle distance coincide.
elements.sent-2411-025: At this temperature, the wave functions of individual particles overlap and begin to synchronize.
elements.sent-2411-026: Unexpectedly, this same critical temperature appears to apply to typical temperatures and densities in astrophysical objects, particularly in some neutron and boson stars \cite{gruber .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These stars are self-gravitating compact objects thought to be made of complex scalar or massive vector fields \cite{schunck2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Gravitationally bounded BE condensates are also considered as potential candidates for dark matter \cite{ Sin:1992bg,Boehmer:2007um (see also \cite{Sharma:2022jio,Aswathi:2023zzn and references therein).}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Recently, the theory of dark matter superfluidity, involving a certain BE transition, has also attracted much attention \cite{Sharma:2022jio,Khoury:2021tvy,Lombardo:2000ec .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Both dark matter and meson BE condensates can significantly influence the properties of compact stars.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, the presence of BEC affects their equation of state (EoS) and, consequently, their mass-to-radius relationship.
elements.sent-2411-032: The superfluidity associated with BEC reduces the viscosity of the superfluid component of the star and influences the heat and angular momentum transport within them (see e.g. \cite{manuel2014 ).}
elements.sent-2411-033: But, apart from extremely large densities, neutron stars are also characterized by their large angular frequencies, which can reach up to $10^3$ Hz.
elements.sent-2411-034: It is known that rotation also affects the thermodynamic properties of neutron stars \cite{hartle1968,sadooghi2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Motivated by the above facts, in this paper, we study the effect of rotation on BEC and the related thermodynamic quantities.
elements.sent-2411-036: To facilitate the field-theoretical investigation of rotation, it is assumed that the system experiences rigid rotation.
elements.sent-2411-037: Inspired by the notion that large angular velocities, up to $10^{22}$ Hz, are realized in heavy-ion collisions and assuming that the quark-gluon plasma produced in these collisions undergoes rigid rotation \cite{becattini2016 , researchers investigate how rotation affects the thermodynamic properties of fermionic systems.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, the impact of rotation on chiral \cite{sadooghi2021 and confinement/deconfinement phase transitions \cite{Chernodub:2020qah is currently a subject of research.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In\cite{chernodub2023-2 , the properties of a spin-one gluon gas are studied under rigid rotation, and the notions of supervorticity and negative Barnett effect are introduced.}
elements.sent-2411-040: They are mainly related to the temperature dependence of the moment of inertia of the gluon gas.
elements.sent-2411-041: The latter is shown to change its sign with increasing temperature.
elements.sent-2411-042: While it is negative below a certain supervortical temperature $T_{s}$, it becomes positive at temperatures larger than $T_{s}$.
elements.sent-2411-043: In \cite{ambrus2023 , it is shown that imaginary angular velocity leads to the appearance of fractal thermodynamics and ninionic statistics in a two dimensional Bose gas bounded to a ring and results in stable ghost-like excitations.}
elements.sent-2411-044: In \cite{sadooghi2024 , the thermodynamic properties of a rigidly rotating Bose gas are studied.}
elements.sent-2411-045: Using the free propagator of a scalar charged field, the full thermodynamic potential, including the zeroth and the first perturbative and nonperturbative ring corrections, is determined.
elements.sent-2411-046: The full pressure arising from this calculation yields various thermodynamic quantities associated with a rigidly rotating Bose gas.
elements.sent-2411-047: In particular, it is shown that under certain circumstance, negative moment of inertia and superluminal sound velocities arise.
elements.sent-2411-048: These properties are interpreted as thermodynamic instabilities appearing at high temperatures in a strongly interacting Bose gas under rigid rotation.
elements.sent-2411-049: We consider only the contribution of particles, and determine first the thermal pressure and the EoS of a slowly rotating Bose gas in two nonrelativistic (NR) and ultrarelativistic (UR) limits.
elements.sent-2411-050: We use the same field theoretical methods as are introduced in \cite{kapusta1979,haber1981,haber1982, kapusta-book .}
elements.sent-2411-051: We show that in both limits, rigid rotation decreases the speed of sound.
elements.sent-2411-052: We then focus on BEC and determine, in particular, its critical temperature and the condensate fraction.
elements.sent-2411-053: We demonstrate that comparing to the critical temperature of BE transition of a nonrotating Bose gas, the critical temperature of a rotating gas is lower, although by increasing the angular velocity the transition temperature increases.
elements.sent-2411-054: We determine the dependence of the fugacities associated with a rotating Bose gas in NR and UR limits on temperature and angular velocities.
elements.sent-2411-055: Using these results, we derive analytical expressions for the pressure, heat capacity, and angular momentum density at temperatures both below and above the critical temperature of BEC.
elements.sent-2411-056: According to our results, the thermodynamic properties of a rotating Bose gas in nonrelativistic limit is similar to an ultrarelativistic gas in the absence of rotation.
elements.sent-2411-057: In particular, the temperature dependence of the heat capacity in a nonrelativistic Bose gas under rotation exhibits a discontinuity at the critical temperature, similar to the one that occurs in ultrarelativistic Bose gases without rotation.
elements.sent-2411-058: We compute the latent heat necessary to convert the condensate to thermal phase at the critical temperature and show that in a rotating Bose gas, it is, in general, larger than the latent heat of a Bose gas in the absence of rotation.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is important to note that the effect of fast rotation on BEC that occurs in an anharmonic trap has been previously studied in \cite{cond2006 .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The anharmonicity is introduced by a potential including a quadratic and a quartic terms.
elements.sent-2411-061: Although the main framework and the way the rotation is introduced in \cite{cond2006 is completely different from the method used in the present paper, our findings partly coincide with the results presented in \cite{cond2006 .}}

elements.sent-2411-323: In the present work, we studied the impact of rigid rotation on the BE transition in nonrelativistic and ultrarelativistic Bose gases.
elements.sent-2411-324: To this purpose, we introduced the rotation in the Lagrangian density of a free complex scalar field and computed the grand canonical partition function of this model at finite temperature and density.
elements.sent-2411-325: By assuming slow rotation, we were able to perform the summation over the quantum number $\ell$ that arises due to the rotational motion and the formulation of the problem in a cylindrical coordinate system.
elements.sent-2411-326: This method enabled us to derive analytical results for the pressure, $P$, energy and number densities, $\epsilon$ and $n$ and compare them with the corresponding results for nonrotating Bose gases in NR and UR limits.
elements.sent-2411-327: We investigated, in particular, how the critical temperature of the BE transition, as well as the condensate and thermal fractions, are affected by rigid rotation.
elements.sent-2411-328: We derived analytical expressions for these quantities and compared them with the corresponding expressions for a nonrotating Bose gas in both NR and UR limits.
elements.sent-2411-329: Our findings revealed that the critical exponents associated with the condensate and thermal fractions differ from those in a nonrotating Bose gas.
